questions.text: What was the primary purpose of ARPANET when it was originally designed by the U.S. Department of Defense?

options.text: To give university researchers a fast way to share academic papers
To build a communication network that could survive a nuclear attack by having no single point of failure
To develop a commercial network that companies could pay to use
To create the World Wide Web for sharing documents globally

questions.explanation: Explanation: ARPANET was funded by the Department of Defense specifically to address the Cold War fear that a Soviet nuclear strike could destroy central communication hubs. The solution was a distributed network - no central hub, multiple paths, any node can route messages to any other. If one node is destroyed, the network heals itself. This is why decentralization was not just a feature - it was the entire point. Note: The Web was invented 20 years later by Tim Berners-Lee; ARPANET had nothing to do with it.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation: ARPANET was funded by the Department of Defense specifically to address the Cold War fear that a Soviet nuclear strike could destroy central communication hubs. The solution was a distributed network - no central hub, multiple paths, any node can route messages to any other. If one node is destroyed, the network heals itself. This is why decentralization was not just a feature - it was the entire point. Note: The Web was invented 20 years later by Tim Berners-Lee; ARPANET had nothing to do with it.

questions.text: In packet switching, which of the following statements is CORRECT?

options.text: All packets of a single message must follow the exact same route to the destination
A dedicated line is reserved for the entire duration of data transfer
Data is split into small packets; each packet can take a different path and they are reassembled at the destination
Packet switching requires a central server to coordinate all traffic

questions.explanation: Explanation: The defining characteristic of packet switching is that data is broken into small packets, each carrying its own header (source IP, destination IP, packet number, TTL). Each packet is an independent entity - it can take any available route. If Route A is congested, the packet takes Route B. They may arrive out of order, but TCP reassembles them in the correct sequence. Option A describes a limitation of circuit switching. Option B is the definition of circuit switching. Option D is the opposite of packet switching's philosophy.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: The defining characteristic of packet switching is that data is broken into small packets, each carrying its own header (source IP, destination IP, packet number, TTL). Each packet is an independent entity - it can take any available route. If Route A is congested, the packet takes Route B. They may arrive out of order, but TCP reassembles them in the correct sequence. Option A describes a limitation of circuit switching. Option B is the definition of circuit switching. Option D is the opposite of packet switching's philosophy.

questions.text: What is the correct relationship between the IP Protocol and TCP Protocol?

options.text: TCP and IP are the same protocol used interchangeably
TCP is a newer and more advanced replacement for IP
IP handles addressing and routing; TCP ensures packets are delivered reliably, in order, without loss
IP works only on local networks; TCP handles long-distance routing

questions.explanation: Explanation: IP and TCP operate at different layers of the TCP/IP stack and serve complementary - not competing - roles. IP (Internet Layer): Assigns addresses to every device, and routers use these addresses to forward packets hop-by-hop from source to destination. IP does NOT guarantee delivery - a packet can be lost and IP won't care. TCP (Transport Layer): Sits above IP. It takes the routed packets and ensures they arrive in correct order, detects missing packets and requests retransmission, and confirms delivery via acknowledgments. Together, they are the backbone of internet communication.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: IP and TCP operate at different layers of the TCP/IP stack and serve complementary - not competing - roles. IP (Internet Layer): Assigns addresses to every device, and routers use these addresses to forward packets hop-by-hop from source to destination. IP does NOT guarantee delivery - a packet can be lost and IP won't care. TCP (Transport Layer): Sits above IP. It takes the routed packets and ensures they arrive in correct order, detects missing packets and requests retransmission, and confirms delivery via acknowledgments. Together, they are the backbone of internet communication.

questions.text: A packet is traveling from Mumbai to Chennai through 30 routers. It starts with TTL = 32. What happens when it reaches the 33rd router?

options.text: The packet is automatically forwarded to the next router with TTL reset to 64
The packet reaches Chennai successfully since 32 hops is enough
The packet never reaches the 33rd router - it is discarded at the 32nd router when TTL reaches 0
The packet is paused and waits for TTL to be refreshed before continuing

questions.explanation: Explanation: TTL starts at an initial value (commonly 64) and decrements by exactly 1 at every router hop. When TTL reaches 0 at any router, that router immediately discards the packet and sends an ICMP "Time Exceeded" error message back to the source. The packet is gone. It never reaches the 33rd router because it is destroyed at the 32nd. In this scenario: TTL = 32 means the packet can survive exactly 32 hops. At hop 32, TTL becomes 0 and the packet is discarded. The traceroute command in your terminal uses this behavior deliberately to map network paths.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: TTL starts at an initial value (commonly 64) and decrements by exactly 1 at every router hop. When TTL reaches 0 at any router, that router immediately discards the packet and sends an ICMP "Time Exceeded" error message back to the source. The packet is gone. It never reaches the 33rd router because it is destroyed at the 32nd. In this scenario: TTL = 32 means the packet can survive exactly 32 hops. At hop 32, TTL becomes 0 and the packet is discarded. The traceroute command in your terminal uses this behavior deliberately to map network paths.

questions.text: What was the significance of January 1, 1983 in internet history?

options.text: The day ARPANET was first switched on and the first message was sent
The day Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web at CERN

questions.explanation: Explanation: Before 1983, different networks used different incompatible protocols (ARPANET used NCP, others used their own). On January 1, 1983 - called "Flag Day" - every ARPANET machine simultaneously switched to TCP/IP. This single moment unified previously isolated networks into one global internetwork. ARPANET went from being "a network" to being just one node in THE internet. This is why January 1, 1983 is called "The Internet's Birthday." ARPANET launched in 1969 (not 1983). The Web was invented in 1989. Google was founded in 1998.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: Before 1983, different networks used different incompatible protocols (ARPANET used NCP, others used their own). On January 1, 1983 - called "Flag Day" - every ARPANET machine simultaneously switched to TCP/IP. This single moment unified previously isolated networks into one global internetwork. ARPANET went from being "a network" to being just one node in THE internet. This is why January 1, 1983 is called "The Internet's Birthday." ARPANET launched in 1969 (not 1983). The Web was invented in 1989. Google was founded in 1998.

questions.text: You are designing a payment system for a bank that must continue processing transactions even if one data center is bombed. Which network design principle, directly inspired by ARPANET's original design, should you apply?

options.text: Build the largest possible central server with maximum redundancy within that single location
Use circuit switching to guarantee each transaction gets a dedicated line
Distribute the system across multiple geographically separated nodes so that losing any single node does not halt operations
Increase TTL values to ensure packets always reach their destination

questions.explanation: Explanation: This question tests whether you can apply ARPANET's design philosophy to a new context - which is exactly what blockchain does. ARPANET's core insight was: distribute across many nodes so no single failure can kill the network. A payment system following this principle would have data centers in Mumbai, Delhi, Bangalore, and Chennai - each fully capable of handling transactions independently. If Mumbai's data center is hit, Delhi continues. This is literally how MST Blockchain works: 71,000+ validators across the globe means no single point of failure. Option A is the opposite (single location = single point of failure). TTL is a packet-level concept unrelated to system architecture.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: This question tests whether you can apply ARPANET's design philosophy to a new context - which is exactly what blockchain does. ARPANET's core insight was: distribute across many nodes so no single failure can kill the network. A payment system following this principle would have data centers in Mumbai, Delhi, Bangalore, and Chennai - each fully capable of handling transactions independently. If Mumbai's data center is hit, Delhi continues. This is literally how MST Blockchain works: 71,000+ validators across the globe means no single point of failure. Option A is the opposite (single location = single point of failure). TTL is a packet-level concept unrelated to system architecture.

questions.text: Statement: "ARPANET was completely decentralized with no central authority controlling it whatsoever."
questions.explanation: Model Justification (Full 2 Marks):While ARPANET's routing architecture was distributed (packets could take multiple paths, no single routing node was essential), the network itself was far from completely decentralized in ownership or governance. The U.S. Department of Defense (specifically DARPA) funded, owned, and controlled ARPANET entirely. They decided who could connect, what research was done, and could shut it down at will. Additionally, ARPANET had a hierarchical structure in practice - some nodes (backbone nodes) were more critical than others. "Decentralized routing" does not mean "decentralized control." This confusion is important for blockchain: a network can have distributed routing (like the internet) but still be controlled by a handful of companies (like today's internet). True decentralization - where no single entity can control or shut down the network - is what blockchain achieves, not what ARPANET achieved.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Verdict: FALSE

Model Justification (Full 2 Marks):

While ARPANET's routing architecture was distributed (packets could take multiple paths, no single routing node was essential), the network itself was far from completely decentralized in ownership or governance. The U.S. Department of Defense (specifically DARPA) funded, owned, and controlled ARPANET entirely. They decided who could connect, what research was done, and could shut it down at will. Additionally, ARPANET had a hierarchical structure in practice - some nodes (backbone nodes) were more critical than others. "Decentralized routing" does not mean "decentralized control." This confusion is important for blockchain: a network can have distributed routing (like the internet) but still be controlled by a handful of companies (like today's internet). True decentralization - where no single entity can control or shut down the network - is what blockchain achieves, not what ARPANET achieved.

1 Mark: Correctly states FALSE and mentions DoD ownership/control
+1 Mark: Explains distinction between distributed routing and decentralized control, and/or connects to blockchain context

questions.text: Statement: "When you send a WhatsApp message, the router in Delhi knows the complete end-to-end path the packets will take from Delhi all the way to the destination server in Singapore, before forwarding them."
questions.explanation: Model Justification (Full 2 Marks):Routers use a system called hop-by-hop forwarding - each router only knows the next step, not the full path. The Delhi router does not know (and does not need to know) the complete route to Singapore. It only consults its local routing table to determine: "Which neighboring router should I send this packet to, to get it closer to the destination?" It forwards the packet to the best neighbor, and that neighbor makes the same local decision. This distributed decision-making is exactly what makes the internet resilient - if the Delhi router knew the complete path and that path broke, it would be stuck. Instead, it just asks its neighbors which way is best, right now, dynamically. If it required every router to know every possible destination, the routing tables would be impossibly large and the system would be centralized.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Verdict: FALSE

Model Justification (Full 2 Marks):

Routers use a system called hop-by-hop forwarding - each router only knows the next step, not the full path. The Delhi router does not know (and does not need to know) the complete route to Singapore. It only consults its local routing table to determine: "Which neighboring router should I send this packet to, to get it closer to the destination?" It forwards the packet to the best neighbor, and that neighbor makes the same local decision. This distributed decision-making is exactly what makes the internet resilient - if the Delhi router knew the complete path and that path broke, it would be stuck. Instead, it just asks its neighbors which way is best, right now, dynamically. If it required every router to know every possible destination, the routing tables would be impossibly large and the system would be centralized.

1 Mark: Correctly states FALSE and mentions hop-by-hop forwarding
+1 Mark: Explains WHY this design is beneficial (resilience, scalability) and how routing tables work locally
